text: The surgeon introduces a secondary trocar under laparoscopic vision to avoid damaging the bowel and other structures. The bowel is carefully pushed away from the operative site. The uterus with bilateral fallopian tubes is visible, and gentle pressure is applied to the left fallopian tube to expose the para-ovarian cyst.

The para-ovarian cyst is visible, appearing large and simple. The surgeon cauterizes a small portion to prevent bleeding, then makes an incision in the cyst wall using scissors. Fluid from the cyst is drained with a suction cannula, completely emptying it. The remaining cyst wall is excised with scissors, and hemostasis is achieved using electrocautery to prevent further bleeding.

So thus this procedure is carried out by a minimally invasive technique of laparoscopy which is now the advanced technology. Thus causing only mild postoperative pain for the patient and early recovery of the patient.
The surgeon secures hemostasis around the para-ovarian cyst using cautery before completely excising the cyst wall laparoscopically.
